suggested.title: [Look Together] Images of ancient temples, statues, and artwork

competence_markers.description: The student can retell the Epic of Gilgamesh with specific detail and offer their own interpretation of its meaning
The student can describe Egyptian religion — Ma'at, the judgment of the dead, the Osiris myth — with accuracy
The student can name and describe several Greek gods and explain why they were not morally perfect
The student can retell the Prometheus story and explain why it matters
The student understands hubris as the Greek concept of overstepping proper limits
The student is aware of Zoroastrianism and its influence on later religions
The student understands that ancient religions were serious worldviews, not failed attempts at modern religion
The student can compare at least one ancient story with a story from an Abrahamic tradition

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of Gilgamesh.
narration_prompts.detail: What did he want? What happened? What do you think the story means?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the Egyptian judgment of the dead.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened when a person died? What was the test? What do you think about a test based on how you lived rather than what you believed?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a Greek god and tell me their story.
narration_prompts.detail: What were they like? What did they control? Were they good?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about Prometheus and the gift of fire.
narration_prompts.detail: What did he do? What happened to him? Why do you think this story matters?
Give me an example from a Greek myth. Why did the Greeks think it was the worst thing you could do?
What did Zarathustra teach? How did his ideas influence the religions that came after?

cross_connections.description: Mesopotamia, Egypt, Greece, and Rome — the civilizations behind the gods.
These ancient traditions are worldviews that answer the big questions.
The relationship between ancient religions and the Abrahamic traditions that grew from them.
Norse mythology as another ancient tradition with similar structures.
The divine Pharaoh, the god-kings of Mesopotamia — religion and political authority intertwined from the beginning.
The extraordinary art produced by these civilizations in service of their gods.

content.why_this_matters:
Long before the religions you may know best — Christianity, Judaism, Islam — existed, people worshipped other gods. In Mesopotamia, the oldest civilization on Earth, people told stories about gods who controlled the rain, the rivers, and the harvests. In Egypt, people believed the Pharaoh was a living god and that the dead could live forever if they passed a test of justice. In Greece and Rome, people worshipped gods who were powerful, beautiful, jealous, petty, and very human in their behavior.

These were not silly beliefs held by confused people. They were serious answers to serious questions — the same questions every culture asks: Why does the sun rise? Why do people die? What is justice? What makes a good life? The people who told these stories built some of the greatest civilizations in history. Their gods were as real to them as anything you believe is to you.

In this packet, you will meet these gods through their stories. You will hear about Gilgamesh, who searched for immortality and failed. About Osiris, who died and was brought back to life. About Prometheus, who stole fire from the gods and gave it to humans. About the scales of Ma'at, where the dead were judged not by who they prayed to but by whether they had lived justly.

These stories are worth knowing — not as myths that were replaced by something better, but as the first great answers to the first great questions.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Awareness that ancient religions were serious** — not childish stories but sophisticated answers to deep questions
2. **The Gilgamesh epic** as the oldest great story — about friendship, loss, and the acceptance of death
3. **Egyptian religion** — Ma'at, judgment after death, the Osiris myth
4. **Greek mythology** — the Olympian gods, their human qualities, and the moral lessons in their stories
5. **The concept of many gods** — polytheism as a worldview, not as a mistake
6. **The moral content of ancient stories** — Prometheus and the gift of fire, the scales of justice, hubris and its consequences
7. **The idea that today's religions grew out of older ones** — that beliefs develop and change over time
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The World Before the God of Abraham

Today, about half the world's population follows a religion that traces back to one figure: Abraham. Jews, Christians, and Muslims all call Abraham their ancestor in faith. But Abraham — if he was a real person — probably lived around 2000 BCE. The civilizations of Mesopotamia and Egypt had been worshipping their gods for more than a thousand years before Abraham was born.

This means that for most of human history, the religions that dominated the world were very different from the ones most people practice today. Instead of one God, people worshipped many gods. Instead of holy books with clear rules, people had myths — great stories that explained the world and taught how to live.

These ancient religions were not failed attempts to discover the "right" religion. They were complete, functioning worldviews that organized some of the most impressive civilizations ever built. Understanding them is not just interesting — it is necessary if you want to understand where the later religions came from.

### Part 2: Mesopotamia — The Oldest Stories in the World

The civilizations of Mesopotamia — Sumer, Babylon, Assyria — produced the oldest written stories we have. Their gods were powerful beings who controlled specific parts of the world:

**Anu** — the god of the sky, the highest authority
**Enlil** — the god of air and storms, who commanded the other gods
**Enki (Ea)** — the god of wisdom and water, clever and often kind to humans
**Inanna (Ishtar)** — the goddess of love and war, fierce and beautiful and unpredictable
**Marduk** — the patron god of Babylon, who became king of the gods
**Shamash** — the sun god, who was also the god of justice

The Mesopotamian gods were not perfectly good. They could be generous or cruel, wise or petty. They created humans not out of love but because they needed servants — someone to do the work of feeding the gods through sacrifices and maintaining their temples. This is a very different picture of humanity than the one in Genesis, where humans are made "in God's image." In Mesopotamia, humans were made to work.

### Part 3: The Epic of Gilgamesh — The Oldest Great Story

Gilgamesh was a king of the city of Uruk, possibly a real person who lived around 2700 BCE. His story was told and retold for centuries before being written down on clay tablets. It is the oldest work of literature in the world.

**The story:** Gilgamesh is powerful but arrogant. The gods create Enkidu — a wild man from the wilderness — to be his match. They fight, then become best friends. Together they go on adventures: they kill the monster Humbaba and the Bull of Heaven.

But Enkidu dies. Gilgamesh is destroyed by grief. He cannot accept that his friend is gone, and he cannot accept that he will die too. So he goes on a quest to find the secret of eternal life.

He finds Utnapishtim — a man who survived a great flood (a story remarkably similar to Noah's story in Genesis, but older). Utnapishtim tells him there is a plant at the bottom of the sea that grants youth. Gilgamesh finds it. A snake steals it while he sleeps.

Gilgamesh goes home empty-handed. He does not find immortality. Instead, he finds something else: the walls of his city, the work of his hands, the legacy of what he built. The story says: you cannot escape death. What you can do is build something worthy of your life.

### Part 4: Egypt — The Gift of the Nile and the Judgment of the Dead

Egyptian religion lasted for over 3,000 years — longer than Christianity has existed. It was organized around a profound idea: Ma'at.

**Ma'at** was not just a goddess — she was a principle: truth, justice, balance, cosmic order. Everything in the universe was supposed to be in balance. The Pharaoh's job was to maintain Ma'at. A person's job was to live according to Ma'at. And when you died, your heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at.

**The Judgment of the Dead:** When an Egyptian died, they believed their heart would be placed on a scale opposite the feather of Ma'at. If your heart was lighter than the feather — meaning you had lived justly, honestly, and in balance — you passed into the afterlife, into the beautiful Field of Reeds. If your heart was heavy with wrongdoing, it was devoured by Ammit, a terrifying creature that was part crocodile, part lion, part hippopotamus.

This is one of the oldest ideas of justice after death in the world. Notice: the test was not "did you pray to the right god?" The test was "did you live justly?"

**The Osiris Myth:** Osiris was the god of agriculture and the underworld. His brother Set murdered him and scattered his body. Osiris's wife, Isis, gathered the pieces and brought him back to life. Their son, Horus, grew up and defeated Set. Osiris became lord of the dead, and Horus became king.

This story — a god who dies and rises again — is one of the oldest stories in the world. When you encounter the Christian story of death and resurrection, you will notice the structural similarity. What this means is debated by scholars, and the student should know that the debate exists.

### Part 5: Greece — Gods Who Were Like People

The Greek gods were extraordinary beings — immortal, powerful, beautiful. But they were not perfectly good. They were jealous, angry, lustful, and proud. They fought with each other, tricked each other, and sometimes treated humans cruelly.

**Zeus** — king of the gods, ruler of the sky and thunder. Powerful but not always wise.
**Hera** — queen of the gods, goddess of marriage. Often angry at Zeus's unfaithfulness.
**Poseidon** — god of the sea. Unpredictable, like the ocean itself.
**Athena** — goddess of wisdom and strategy. Born fully grown from Zeus's head.
**Apollo** — god of the sun, music, and healing. The most rational of the gods.
**Artemis** — goddess of the hunt and the wilderness. Fierce and independent.
**Aphrodite** — goddess of love and beauty. Irresistible and dangerous.
**Hermes** — messenger of the gods, god of travelers and tricksters.
**Dionysus** — god of wine, celebration, and the breaking of boundaries.

**Why did the Greeks worship gods who were not perfectly good?** Because the world they saw was not perfectly good either. Storms destroyed ships. Disease killed children. Wars destroyed cities. The Greek gods matched the world the Greeks actually experienced — powerful forces that were not evil but were not reliably on your side either.

### Part 6: Prometheus — The Gift of Fire

One of the most important Greek myths: Prometheus, a Titan (an older race of gods), stole fire from Mount Olympus and gave it to human beings. Zeus was furious. He punished Prometheus by chaining him to a rock, where an eagle ate his liver every day — and every night, it grew back, so the torment never ended.

Why is this story important? Because fire represents everything that makes civilization possible — warmth, cooking, metalworking, light in the darkness. Prometheus gave humans the means to build everything they would ever build. And the price was terrible.

The story says something profound about the human condition: the gifts that make us powerful come at a cost. Knowledge is a gift and a burden. Power is useful and dangerous. This is one of the oldest observations about human nature, and it appears in many traditions — the biblical story of Adam and Eve eating the fruit of knowledge has a similar structure.

### Part 7: Hubris — The Sin the Greeks Feared Most

The Greeks had a word for the worst thing a person could do: hubris. It meant overstepping your proper limits — acting as though you were a god when you were only human.

The stories are full of it. Icarus flew too close to the sun on wings of wax, and the wax melted, and he fell. Arachne boasted that she could weave better than the goddess Athena, and was turned into a spider. Narcissus fell in love with his own reflection and wasted away.

The lesson: know your limits. The universe has a shape, and if you try to exceed your place in it, you will be destroyed. This is a very different moral framework from the Abrahamic traditions, which say "obey God's commands." The Greek framework says: "know what you are, and do not pretend to be more."

### Part 8: Rome — The Practical Religion

The Romans borrowed most of their gods from the Greeks — Zeus became Jupiter, Hera became Juno, Athena became Minerva, Ares became Mars. But Roman religion had a different feel. It was practical. The Romans did not worship their gods out of love or fear — they worshipped them the way you might maintain a contract. "We give you sacrifices. You give us good harvests and victory in war."

Roman religion was also deeply civic — it was part of being a Roman citizen. Every family had household gods (the Lares and Penates). The calendar was full of religious festivals. The state itself was protected by the gods, and maintaining the right relationship with the gods was a matter of national security.

This is why the Romans had trouble with Christians and Jews: not because Romans cared what you believed in private, but because Christians and Jews refused to participate in the public religious life that Romans considered essential for the welfare of the state.

### Part 9: Fire Temples and the Battle Between Good and Evil — Zoroastrianism

In ancient Persia (modern Iran), a prophet named Zarathustra — known in the West as Zoroaster — taught something that would change the world. He taught that the universe is a battleground between good and evil — between a supreme good god, Ahura Mazda (the Wise Lord), and an evil spirit, Angra Mainyu (the Destructive Spirit).

Every person must choose a side. Good thoughts, good words, good deeds — these are the weapons in the cosmic battle. After death, each person is judged. The righteous cross a bridge into paradise. The wicked fall.

This may sound familiar. The concept of a cosmic battle between good and evil, a judgment after death, a paradise for the righteous — these ideas were not originally Abrahamic. They came from Zoroastrianism. When the Jewish people lived under Persian rule, they encountered these ideas, and many scholars believe these ideas deeply influenced Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. The devil, angels and demons, heaven and hell, the final judgment — all of these may have Zoroastrian roots.

Today, Zoroastrianism is a small religion — but its influence on the traditions that followed it is enormous.

### Part 10: The Stories Continue

The ancient religions did not simply disappear. They were gradually replaced — by Christianity in the Roman Empire, by Islam in Persia, by new traditions in other places. But the stories survived. The myths of Greece and Rome have been told and retold for over 2,000 years. The Epic of Gilgamesh was rediscovered in the 19th century and astonished the world. Egyptian religion continues to fascinate millions.

Why do these stories survive? Because they are about things that never change — love, death, courage, justice, the search for meaning, the acceptance of limits. The gods of Olympus and the gods of Mesopotamia may no longer be worshipped, but the questions they answered are still our questions.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Read-Through [Ongoing]
Read Greek myths from D'Aulaires', Egyptian myths, and the Gilgamesh retelling aloud. After each, the student narrates back what they heard.

### Study 2: Gilgamesh [Early Packet]
Read the Gilgamesh story in full. Discuss: What did Gilgamesh want? Did he get it? What did he learn instead? Why do you think this story has lasted for over 4,000 years?

### Study 3: The Scales of Ma'at [Mid-Packet]
Study the Egyptian judgment of the dead using images and text. Discuss: If your heart were weighed against the feather of Ma'at, what would matter? Not what you believed — but how you lived. How is this different from or similar to how other traditions think about what happens after death?

### Study 4: The Greek Gods [Mid-Packet]
Pick three Greek gods and learn their stories. For each: What did they control? What were they like? Were they good? The student should notice that Greek gods were powerful but not morally perfect — and discuss what that means.

### Study 5: Prometheus and Adam [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Compare the Prometheus story with the story of Adam and Eve. Both are about humans gaining knowledge. Both involve a cost. What is similar? What is different? What does each story say about whether knowledge is a gift or a curse?

### Study 6: Zoroastrianism's Influence [Late Packet]
Introduce Zoroastrianism and its key ideas: the battle between good and evil, judgment after death, paradise. Then discuss: many of these ideas appear in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. What does it mean that ideas travel between religions? Does it make the later religions less true? More true? Something else?

### Study 7: The Story Wall [End of Packet]
Create a display showing the gods and stories from each ancient tradition studied. For each, note: what did these people believe? What questions were they trying to answer? What do their stories tell us about what they valued?

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Story Narration
**Developing well:** The student can retell ancient stories with specific detail — names, events, consequences. They remember which story belongs to which tradition.
**Developing slowly:** Stories blur together or the student cannot retell any specific story with detail.

### 2. Moral Engagement
**Developing well:** The student engages with the moral content of the stories: Gilgamesh's grief, the scales of Ma'at, the consequences of hubris. They offer their own interpretations.
**Developing slowly:** The student treats the stories as entertainment without engaging with their meaning.

### 3. Cross-Tradition Awareness
**Developing well:** The student notices connections between traditions — the flood story in Gilgamesh and Genesis, the death-and-resurrection motif in Osiris and Christianity, the fire-and-knowledge theme in Prometheus and Adam and Eve.
**Developing slowly:** Each tradition exists in isolation without connections.

### 4. Respect for Ancient Traditions
**Developing well:** The student speaks of ancient traditions with respect. "The Egyptians believed..." rather than "The Egyptians just thought..."
**Developing slowly:** Dismissive language — "that's silly" or "they didn't know about the real God."
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Greek Myth Dominance
Greek mythology is the best-resourced ancient tradition in children's literature. It is easy for Greece to consume the entire packet. Guard against this. Gilgamesh, Egyptian religion, and Zoroastrianism each need genuine time and attention. A student who "did ancient religions" but can only name Greek gods has missed the most important material.

### Treating Death as Too Dark
The Gilgamesh epic is about mortality. Egyptian religion is about judgment after death. These themes may feel heavy for young students. They are not too dark — they are honest. Children think about death. The ancient stories give them a language for it. Do not sanitize the material. A six-year-old can hear that Gilgamesh was sad when his friend died and went looking for a way to live forever. They can handle it — and they will be better for having encountered it.

### Missing the Zoroastrian Influence
Zoroastrianism is the most commonly overlooked tradition in this packet, but it may be the most important for understanding the development of the Abrahamic traditions. Give it real time. The idea that concepts like the devil, heaven and hell, and the final judgment may have traveled from Persia into Judaism is one of 1.3's most important observations.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Gilgamesh
**Strong:** Retells the story with key elements — Enkidu, the friendship, the death, the quest for immortality, the failure, the return. Offers an interpretation of what the story means.
**Weak:** "A king went on a journey." No detail, no engagement.

### Prompt 2: Egyptian Judgment
**Strong:** Describes the scales, the feather, and the test with accuracy. Engages with the idea that the test is about how you lived.
**Weak:** "They weighed your heart." No elaboration.

### Prompt 5: Hubris
**Strong:** Defines hubris in their own words, gives a specific example from a myth, and explains why the Greeks feared it.
**Weak:** "It means being proud." Incomplete.
